fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to put together that shared plumbing library we keep talking about for the annotation-driven framework. Right now every app team is doing their own thing for basic stuff — figuring out where their classes live, running startup steps in the right order, turning handler responses into something the client can actually read, and dealing with messy incoming request values. It's causing inconsistencies and a bunch of copy-paste bugs.

Basically we need four or five small building blocks. One for reading a project config and knowing which packages to scan (the convention stuff we agreed on, like we did for the login module layout — check how that was structured). One for running async startup tasks one-at-a-time so nothing fires out of order. Something to track what kind of value a handler actually returned so downstream logic knows what to do with it. Then serialisation — both the happy-path response and error/status outcomes — into whatever format the client asked for (we're supporting at least two formats). And finally a converter for raw incoming text params so handlers get proper typed values and bad input doesn't blow up with internal stack traces leaking out.

Errors should never expose internal runtime details to the client, ever. Keep output consistent across all the formatters. The async piece especially needs to be rock-solid — order must be guaranteed. Let me know if anything's unclear, I'm around.

trigger_keywords: what suffixes are appended to the base package
how are package names derived from base config
what are the conventional package suffixes for each role
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The four conventional suffixes are: '.controllers' for the controller role, '.verticles' for the verticle/component role, '.fixtures' for the fixture role, and '.domains' for the domain role. These are appended directly to the value of the 'src-package' key in the config descriptor.

trigger_keywords: which roles are multi-valued lists vs single values
are all package locations lists or strings
which config fields accept arrays vs a single string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Controller packages ('controller-packages') and fixture packages ('fixture-packages') are multi-valued — they hold a list of package strings and are joined with commas in output. Verticle package ('verticle-package') and domain package ('domain-package') are single-valued strings.

trigger_keywords: what does the async chain output look like
how is the task execution order formatted
what gets printed after the sequential chain completes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The output has three lines: 'order=<accumulated string>' where each task appends its zero-based index followed by a semicolon (e.g. '0;1;2;'), then 'length=<count>' for the number of tasks scheduled, then 'completed=yes' if the chain finished successfully. An empty task list yields 'order=' with an empty string.

trigger_keywords: what is the empty/initial type reported by the payload holder
what sentinel value does a new payload holder report
what type string is shown before a value is set
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A freshly created payload holder reports the sentinel type token 'Void' (capital V). After a value is set, it reports the concrete type: 'Dog' for an object value with name and breed attributes, or 'String' for a scalar/text value. Both the initial type and the post-set type are printed, one per line as 'type=...'.

trigger_keywords: what fields does a domain entity serialise to JSON
what properties appear in the JSON body for a Dog entity
what does the domain object JSON output include
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A domain entity (Dog) serialises all bean-style properties in this order: 'age' (null when not assigned), 'name' (string), 'breed' (string), 'puppy' (boolean, defaults to false). The JSON body is compact with no extra whitespace, e.g. {"age":null,"name":"Snoopy","breed":"Beagle","puppy":false}.

trigger_keywords: how is an unexpected/internal error serialised to JSON
what does the error body look like when no status code is given
does the unexpected error response include a status line in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For an unexpected internal failure, the output is: 'media_type=application/json', then 'body={"error":{"code":500,"message":"Internal Server Error"}}'. Crucially, there is NO 'status=...' line in the output — the status line only appears for explicit HTTP status outcomes (where a code and message are provided). The message must be exactly 'Internal Server Error' and must not leak any detail of the underlying failure.

trigger_keywords: how is boolean conversion handled
what text values map to true vs false in param conversion
what is the rule for converting a string to boolean
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Boolean conversion uses a strict literal match: only the exact string 'true' resolves to true. Any other value — including 'True', 'TRUE', '1', 'yes', or anything else — resolves to false. There is no error/rejection for booleans; any input produces a valid boolean result.

trigger_keywords: what happens when an unsupported type is passed to the converter
how does the converter handle unknown target types
what is the output when the type token is not recognised
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the declared target type is not one of the supported tokens (string, integer, long, float, boolean), the converter yields no value. The output is 'target=<token>' followed by 'value=null'. It does NOT produce an error category — only unsupported types yield 'value=null'; malformed numeric input yields 'error=number_format'.

trigger_keywords: what is the plain text format for an HTTP status outcome
how is a status code rendered in text/plain marshalling
what separator is used between status code and message in text output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a plain-text status outcome, the body is a single line combining the numeric code and the message separated by ' : ' (space-colon-space), for example '406 : Not acceptable'. The output also includes 'media_type=text/plain' and 'status=<code>'. A non-text payload (e.g. a numeric value) cannot be marshalled to text and produces 'error=unmarshallable_text' with no media_type line.

trigger_keywords: what error category is used for bad numeric param input
how is a parse failure reported in param conversion
what string is output when a number cannot be parsed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a textual value cannot be parsed as the requested numeric type (integer, long, or float), the output is 'target=<type>' followed by 'error=number_format'. The exact neutral category string is 'number_format' (underscore, all lowercase). No runtime exception detail or message is ever leaked in the output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'config', 'chain', 'payload', 'marshal_json', 'marshal_status', 'convert', 'marshal_text'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the convention stuff we agreed on, like we did for the login module layout — check how that was structured
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The package layout resolution logic in Feature 1: appending fixed suffixes (.controllers, .verticles, .fixtures, .domains) to the 'src-package' base value, with explicit keys ('controller-packages', 'verticle-package', 'fixture-packages', 'domain-package') overriding the derived values. See feature1_config_layout.json and the config descriptor handling in the core library.

context_pointers.vague_reference: serialisation into whatever format the client asked for (we're supporting at least two formats)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Two marshalling paths are required: JSON (media type 'application/json', covering domain entities, plain objects/arrays, and error/status bodies) as specified in Features 4 and 5, and plain-text (media type 'text/plain', covering string values and status lines, rejecting non-text payloads) as specified in Feature 7.

context_pointers.vague_reference: errors should never expose internal runtime details to the client, ever
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Three specific neutralisation rules: (1) numeric parse failures in param conversion must output 'error=number_format' not the exception message; (2) unexpected server errors in JSON marshalling must output the fixed string 'Internal Server Error' with code 500, not any exception detail; (3) non-text payloads in plain-text marshalling must output 'error=unmarshallable_text' not any type or value detail.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the specific detail on that off the top of my head — you'll probably need to dig into the existing test cases in the rcb_tests/public_test_cases folder or check with the original engineer who scoped this out. The key thing from my side is that behaviour stays consistent and nothing internal leaks to clients.

feature1_config_layout.json.description: Resolve the component package layout of an annotation-driven web application from a JSON configuration descriptor. The descriptor may provide a single base package; in that case the locations of the controller, verticle/component, fixture and domain packages are each derived by appending a fixed conventional suffix to that base. The descriptor may instead (or additionally) provide explicit package locations for any of these roles, and any explicitly provided value overrides the convention-derived one. Controller and fixture locations are multi-valued lists; the verticle/component and domain locations are single values. The output reports each resolved location.

feature2_async_chaining.json.description: Run an ordered list of asynchronous one-shot tasks strictly in sequence, where each task signals its own completion before the next is allowed to begin. The input gives the number of tasks to schedule; each task appends its zero-based position followed by a semicolon to a shared accumulator and then completes. Because the chain is sequential, the accumulator always lists the positions in ascending order regardless of how long any individual task takes. The output reports the accumulated order string, the number of tasks, and whether the chain finished successfully. An empty task list completes immediately with an empty order string.

feature3_payload_type.json.description: Track the runtime type carried by a response payload holder. A freshly created holder is empty and reports a sentinel empty type. Once a value is placed into the holder, it reports the concrete type of that value instead. The output prints the reported type immediately after creation and, when a value is supplied, again after the value has been set, so the transition from empty to typed is observable. A supplied object value with name and breed attributes is treated as a domain entity; a supplied scalar value is treated as a text value.

feature4_json_marshalling.json.description: Serialise a response payload to a JSON representation under the JSON content type. A domain entity is serialised field by field, exposing all of its bean-style properties (including null and boolean-valued ones) as JSON members. A structured object value is serialised as a JSON object and a structured array value as a JSON array, each reproduced faithfully. The output reports the negotiated media type and the serialised body.

feature5_json_status_marshalling.json.description: Render an error response body in the JSON format. For an explicit HTTP status outcome the body wraps an error object carrying the numeric status code and the associated status message. For an unexpected internal failure rendered without detail disclosure, the body wraps an error object carrying the internal-server-error code and a generic message that does not leak any implementation detail of the failure. The output reports the media type, the status code when applicable, and the serialised error body.

feature6_param_conversion.json.description: Convert a raw textual request parameter into a typed value according to a declared target type. Supported target types include text (passed through unchanged), 32-bit and 64-bit integers, floating point, and boolean (any value other than the literal true text resolves to false). When the textual value cannot be parsed as the requested numeric type, the conversion is rejected and reported as a neutral numeric-format error category rather than leaking any runtime-specific failure detail. When the declared target type is one the converter does not support, it yields no value. The output reports the requested target type and either the converted value or the neutral error category.

feature7_plaintext_marshalling.json.description: Serialise a response payload under the plain-text content type. A text value is emitted verbatim as the body. An explicit HTTP status outcome is rendered as a single line combining the numeric status code and the status message. A non-text payload cannot be represented in this format, so the attempt is rejected and reported as a neutral unmarshallable-text error category rather than leaking any runtime-specific failure detail. The output reports the media type and either the body, the status line, or the neutral error category.
